planting.note: Plant container vines or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; woolly Dutchman's pipe is the southeastern-native species of Aristolochia, hardier and better adapted to Gulf Coast conditions than A. macrophylla.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun with a sturdy support structure; woolly Dutchman's pipe is the southeastern US native species of Aristolochia, distinguished from A. macrophylla by its dense woolly coating of soft hairs on the leaves and stems — a beautiful textural detail. Like all native Aristolochia species, it is the exclusive larval host plant for the pipevine swallowtail butterfly (Battus philenor), one of the most spectacular black-and-iridescent-blue native butterflies in eastern North America. A single established vine supports dozens of caterpillars each season. The unusual curved, yellowish-green to brown pipe-shaped flowers are insect-trapping devices that temporarily trap small flies for pollination. Twining stems reach 10–20 feet and provide rapid screening in partial shade. Highly recommended for butterfly gardens in zones 5–9, particularly in the Southeast where this species is native.

planting.note: Plant crown divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; set the crown at soil level and do not bury; lady fern is the most adaptable and widely grown native fern and succeeds in a very wide range of moist, shaded conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; lady fern is one of the most beautiful, adaptable, and widely grown native ferns in North America, producing finely divided, arching, bright green to chartreuse fronds in graceful spreading clumps that light up dark garden corners throughout summer. Unlike many ferns it is deciduous, going dormant in winter and re-emerging reliably each spring. It tolerates a wider range of conditions than most ferns — moderately dry soil, some drought once established, and varying light levels from dense shade to dappled sun. Dozens of ornamental cultivars exist, including red-stemmed forms ('Lady in Red'), crested forms, and dwarf selections. It naturalizes readily in moist woodland gardens and combines beautifully with hostas, bleeding heart, and native wildflowers. Division every 3–4 years keeps clumps vigorous.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; sensitive fern spreads vigorously by rhizome in moist conditions and can colonize large areas quickly — site it where spreading is welcome.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist conditions; sensitive fern is one of the most distinctive and bold native ferns of eastern North America, producing large, broadly triangular, undivided-looking fronds with wide, rounded lobes that give it an unusually coarse texture compared to the typically finely cut fern frond. It gets its name from its extreme sensitivity to frost — the fronds collapse at the first light freeze in fall. A separate fertile frond with clusters of bead-like sporangia remains standing through winter and is an attractive structural element in dried arrangements. It spreads readily by rhizome in moist conditions and is excellent for naturalizing wet, shaded areas along stream banks, pond margins, and bog gardens. The coarse, bold foliage provides excellent contrast with fine-textured shade plants.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; netted chain fern requires strongly acidic, boggy conditions and will not establish in neutral or alkaline soils.

directions: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; netted chain fern is a native fern of moist, acidic pocosins, pine flatwoods, bogs, and shaded swamp margins throughout the eastern US coastal plain, immediately distinguished from similar chain ferns by the pattern of small, net-like (areolate) veins between the primary leaf veins on sterile fronds. The sterile fronds are pinnate with broadly winged midribs; the fertile fronds look strikingly different — much narrower and almost completely covered by elongated chain-like sori along each midrib. It spreads by rhizome in wet, acid soil to form attractive colonies. An excellent native fern for bog gardens, wet acidic woodland edges, or naturalized areas with consistently wet, acidic soil in the Southeast and coastal plain.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), wet to moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Virginia chain fern tolerates deeper standing water than most ferns and can colonize the margins of swamps, bogs, and wet ditches.

directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), wet to moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Virginia chain fern is a robust, twice-pinnate native fern of Atlantic coastal plain bogs, swamps, and wet pine flatwoods — larger and more dramatic than the similar netted chain fern, with bipinnate fronds reaching 2–5 feet in saturated acidic soil. The sori are arranged in the chain-like pattern that names the genus, running in long lines parallel to the midrib. It tolerates standing water around the root zone and is one of the best native ferns for establishing in swampy, waterlogged, acidic conditions where few other plants thrive. Spreads steadily by rhizome to form bold colonies in appropriate wet, acid conditions. An important structural plant in wetland restoration and naturalistic bog gardens in the Southeast and coastal plain from New England to Florida.
